Ineg.label: Negative Engagement
Ineg.full_description: The infant is negative, protesting or withdrawn. The infant must display negative facial expressions (e.g., anger, sadness, disgust, distress, cry or grimace faces), and/or whimpering, complaining, fussy, or crying vocalizations. There is no gaze criterion and a variety of gestural and postural behaviors may occur (e.g., pushing the caregiver away, twisting and turning in chair, arching body). Negative Engagement can be divided into two specific phases: Protest and Withdrawn. Always try to code whether the infant is in a Protest or Withdrawn phase. If it is not possible to make this differentiation, code the undifferentiated category of Negative Engagement. The overall feel for this code is unhappy or sad without the further differentiation of the Protest or Withdrawn codes. In typical populations, Protest will be the most common negative code and Withdrawn will be rare.
Ineg.video_description: Look for negative facial expressions such as anger, sadness, disgust, distress, cry faces or grimace faces. Observe gestural and postural behaviors including pushing the caregiver away, twisting and turning in the chair, and arching the body. The infant may display a generally unhappy or sad demeanor. There is no gaze criterion. The overall visual impression is one of distress or unhappiness without the further differentiation into Protest (externalizing) or Withdrawn (internalizing) behaviors.
Ineg.audio_description: Listen for whimpering, complaining, fussy, or crying vocalizations. The infant's vocal tone will convey distress, unhappiness or sadness. These vocalizations indicate negative engagement without further differentiation into the more specific Protest (active crying, fussing) or Withdrawn (quiet whimpering) subcategories.

Ipro.label: Protest
Ipro.full_description: The infant is protesting. The infant often displays facial expressions of anger, frustration, grimaces, and/or is fussing or crying. The infant tends to be active during this phase: the infant may arch his/her back, kick or bang his/her arms against the chair, try to escape/get away, gesture, want to be picked up, bat at the caregiver, or push and pull away from the caregiver. This code captures externalizing type behaviors, and can have an angry or hostile aspect. There is no gaze criterion.
Ipro.video_description: Look for facial expressions of anger, frustration, and grimaces. The infant tends to be physically active during protest: arching the back, kicking or banging arms against the chair, trying to escape or get away, gesturing to be picked up, batting at the caregiver, or pushing and pulling away from the caregiver. This code captures externalizing type behaviors with an angry or hostile visual quality. There is no gaze criterion.
Ipro.audio_description: Listen for fussing, crying, angry vocalizations, or hostile-sounding outbursts. The vocal quality during protest is active and externalizing — the infant is loudly expressing displeasure through crying, screaming, or forceful fussing. The vocalizations often accompany physical protest behaviors.

Iwit.label: Withdrawn
Iwit.full_description: The infant is withdrawn and minimally engaged with the caregiver. This phase often includes sad facial expressions, whimpering vocalizations, slumped posture, listless demeanor, and gaze aversion. The infant typically engages in few activities and gives the impression of being 'spaced out' and disengaged from the caregiver. This code captures internalizing type behaviors. Although the infant often gaze averts, there is no specific gaze criterion defining this phase. Do not code withdrawn if the infant is crying or looking away from the caregiver but is focused on an object.
Iwit.video_description: Look for sad facial expressions, slumped posture, and a listless demeanor. The infant appears 'spaced out' and disengaged from the caregiver, engaging in very few activities. The infant often averts gaze but there is no specific gaze criterion. This code captures internalizing type behaviors. Do not code Withdrawn if the infant is looking away from the caregiver but is clearly focused on an object — that would be Object/Environment Engagement.
Iwit.audio_description: Listen for whimpering, quiet or subdued vocalizations, or near silence. The vocal quality during withdrawal is minimal and internalizing — the infant is not actively crying or fussing but may produce soft, sad sounds. The absence of active vocalization combined with a lack of engagement signals withdrawn behavior. Do not code Withdrawn if the infant is crying.

Inon.label: Object/Environment Engagement
Inon.full_description: The infant is looking at objects that are either proximal (e.g., infant seat) or distal (e.g., camera). The infant may manipulate proximal objects. The infant's eyes must be directed towards an object. The infant's facial expressions are typically interested or neutral but may be on occasion positive. The infant may or may not vocalize. Objects include the infant's hands, feet, belly or clothing; the caregiver's body (e.g., trunk, hands, jewelry); and objects that are part of the laboratory setting (e.g., chair strap, side of the chair, cameras or curtains). The caregiver's face does not constitute an object. If the infant displays negative affect or vocalizations while looking at an object, code Negative Engagement, Protest or Withdrawn. If the infant laughs or smiles while looking at an object, code Object/Environment Engagement not Social Positive Engagement.
Inon.video_description: Look for the infant's eyes directed towards an object — either proximal (infant seat, own hands, feet, belly, clothing, caregiver's body/hands/jewelry) or distal (camera, chair strap, curtains). The infant may manipulate proximal objects. Facial expressions are typically interested or neutral but may occasionally be positive. The caregiver's face does not constitute an object. If the infant displays negative facial affect while looking at an object, code Negative Engagement instead. If the infant laughs or smiles while looking at an object, still code Object/Environment Engagement, not Social Positive Engagement.
Inon.audio_description: The infant may or may not vocalize during Object/Environment Engagement. If vocalizations are present, they are typically neutral or interested in quality — babbling, cooing, or quiet sounds while attending to objects. If the infant displays negative vocalizations (whimpering, crying, fussing) while looking at an object, code Negative Engagement, Protest or Withdrawn instead.

Ineu.label: Social Monitor
Ineu.full_description: The infant's attention is directed towards the caregiver. The infant looks at the adult's face with a neutral or interested facial expression. The infant's eyes must be oriented towards the adult's face and he/she may vocalize in a neutral/positive manner. In cases where it is difficult to differentiate between Negative Engagement (particularly withdrawn) and Social Monitor, score Social Monitor. Similarly, if it is difficult to differentiate between Social Monitor and Social Positive Engagement, score Social Monitor. If it is difficult to differentiate between Object Engagement and Social Monitor, score Object Engagement.
Ineu.video_description: Look for the infant's eyes oriented towards the adult's face with a neutral or interested facial expression. The infant is attending to the caregiver without displaying clear positive affect (smiles, play faces) or negative affect. In ambiguous cases: if it is difficult to differentiate between Withdrawn and Social Monitor, score Social Monitor. If it is difficult to differentiate between Social Monitor and Social Positive Engagement, score Social Monitor. If it is difficult to differentiate between Object Engagement and Social Monitor, score Object Engagement.
Ineu.audio_description: The infant may vocalize in a neutral or positive manner while monitoring the caregiver — such as soft babbling, cooing, or neutral sounds. The vocalizations are not negative (no crying, fussing, whimpering) and not strongly positive (no laughing, squealing). In ambiguous vocal cases, default to Social Monitor over Social Positive Engagement.

Ipos.label: Social Positive Engagement
Ipos.full_description: This scale assesses the extent to which the infant is engaged with the caregiver in an overall positive manner. The infant must display facial expressions of joy particularly smiles, but occasionally coo and play faces. The infant must look towards the caregiver's face. The infant may be vocalizing in a positive manner, laughing, babbling, or squealing. Do not code Social Positive Engagement if the infant vocalizes but does not display a smile face. A smile face includes upturned mouth, crinkly eyes and raised cheeks. The infant may be engaged with the adult in rhythmic social play-games (e.g., pat-a-cake, peek-a-boo). During these games it may not be possible for the infant to look directly at the adult's face because of the caregiver's position (e.g., adult's face buried in infant's lap) or the nature of the game (e.g., face covered in peek-a-boo). Score these instances as Social Positive Engagement as long as the infant's facial and/or vocal expressions are positive and gaze is focused towards the caregiver.
Ipos.video_description: Look for facial expressions of joy — particularly smiles with upturned mouth, crinkly eyes, and raised cheeks. The infant must look towards the caregiver's face. Play faces and coo faces also qualify. The infant may be engaged in rhythmic social play-games (pat-a-cake, peek-a-boo). During these games the infant may not look directly at the adult's face due to positioning, but score Social Positive Engagement as long as the infant's facial expressions are positive and gaze is focused towards the caregiver. Do not code Social Positive Engagement based on vocalizations alone without a visible smile face.
Ipos.audio_description: Listen for positive vocalizations such as laughing, joyful babbling, cooing, or squealing. The vocal quality is clearly positive and engaged. However, do not code Social Positive Engagement based on vocalizations alone — a smile face must also be present visually. Positive vocalizations during social play-games (pat-a-cake, peek-a-boo) support this code when combined with positive facial expressions.

Islp.label: Sleep
Islp.video_description: The infant's eyes are closed and the body is still, indicating sleep.
Islp.audio_description: The infant is silent or producing only quiet breathing sounds consistent with sleep.

Iusc.label: Unscorable
Iusc.full_description: If the baby's face is obscured because of poor camera angles, technical problems, or because the adult is blocking the baby's camera, score Unscorable. If it is possible to see part of the infant's face, code the appropriate engagement phase. Do not guess. Even though the infant's face is blocked, if it is clear that the infant is looking away from the mother, score Object/Environment Engagement. If the infant's face is obscured and the infant is fussing or crying, score the appropriate Negative Engagement code. If the infant's face is obscured and the infant seems to be looking in the direction of the mother, score Unscorable because it will be impossible to know exactly where the infant is looking or to determine the infant's facial expression. However, if the infant does not move and is in the same coding behavior from the beginning of being obscured to being observable then score that coding behavior.
Iusc.video_description: The baby's face is obscured because of poor camera angles, technical problems, or because the adult is blocking the baby's camera. If part of the infant's face is visible, code the appropriate engagement phase instead. If the infant is clearly looking away from the mother even though the face is blocked, score Object/Environment Engagement. If the infant does not move and maintains the same behavior from before being obscured, continue that code.
Iusc.audio_description: If the infant's face is obscured but the infant is fussing or crying, score the appropriate Negative Engagement code based on the audio alone. If no clear audio cues are available and the face is not visible, score Unscorable.

Cneg.label: Negative Engagement
Cneg.full_description: The adult is negative, withdrawn, intrusive or hostile. His/her facial expressions are sad, sober, expressionless, stern, angry or hostile. There are no smiles or hints of smiles. The adult's vocalizations are expressionless, sharp, angry, loud or adultlike. The adult may be silent or speak in a monotone. The adult may be leaning back in his/her chair and appear at a loss of what to do. There is no burst-pause, sing-song, or exaggerated language characteristic of motherese. When considering the adult's vocalizations, pay attention to the affective tone of the vocalizations and disregard the content of the vocalizations. There is no gaze criterion. According to the main affect negative Engagement can be divided into three specific phases: Withdrawn, Intrusive and Hostile. Always try to code whether the adult is in the Withdrawn, Intrusive or Hostile phase. If it is not possible to make this differentiation, code the undifferentiated category of Negative Engagement. If Intrusive and Hostile behavior can be observed at once, code Hostile.
Cneg.video_description: Look for sad, sober, expressionless, stern, angry, or hostile facial expressions. There are no smiles or hints of smiles. The adult may be leaning back in their chair and appear at a loss of what to do. There is no gaze criterion. Try to differentiate between Withdrawn (flat, disengaged posture), Intrusive (tense, overly stimulating behavior), and Hostile (aggressive, upset expressions) subtypes.
Cneg.audio_description: Listen for expressionless, sharp, angry, loud, or adultlike vocalizations. The adult may be silent or speak in a monotone. There is no burst-pause, sing-song, or exaggerated language characteristic of motherese. Pay attention to the affective tone of the vocalizations and disregard the content. The vocal quality conveys negativity, disengagement, or hostility.

Cwit.label: Withdrawn
Cwit.full_description: The adult is minimally engaged and withdrawn with the baby. The adult's facial expressions are sad, flat, or expressionless. There are no smiles or hints of smiles. The adult may be silent, speak or whisper in a flat or expressionless monotone. The adult may be leaning back in his/her chair, not touch the baby or touch it in a mechanical manner, and appear hesitant or at a loss of what to do. There is no burst pause, sing-song, or exaggerated vocalizations (i.e., motherese). There is no gaze criterion.
Cwit.video_description: Look for sad, flat, or expressionless facial expressions with no smiles or hints of smiles. The adult may be leaning back in the chair, not touching the baby or touching mechanically, and appearing hesitant or at a loss of what to do. The caregiver is minimally engaged and visually disengaged. There is no gaze criterion.
Cwit.audio_description: Listen for silence, flat speech, whispered or expressionless monotone vocalizations. There is no burst-pause, sing-song, or exaggerated language characteristic of motherese. The vocal quality is minimal, flat, and disengaged.

Cint.label: Intrusive
Cint.full_description: The adult's engagement with the baby is characterized by intrusive affect/behavior. The adult's facial expressions are tense, stressed or little authentic. The infant is not given the chance to respond to maternal initiatives as these follow one another too fast and do not await infant reactions. Maternal and infant reactions are not well-matched so that for example the mother interrupts the infant's activities in order to pursue her own programme. The mother stimulates the infant excessively without caring about infant signals of discomfort and/or withdrawal. Regardless of the infant's behavior the mother acts too loud, too expressively and too close to her child. The infant's activities are restrained, for example by turning his or her face around in order to make eye contact, by holding tight hands or feet while playing etc.
Cint.video_description: Look for tense, stressed, or inauthentic facial expressions. The caregiver follows initiatives too fast without awaiting infant reactions, interrupts the infant's activities, stimulates the infant excessively, and acts too close to the child. The infant's activities may be physically restrained — the caregiver may turn the infant's face to force eye contact or hold the infant's hands or feet tightly. Maternal and infant reactions appear poorly matched.
Cint.audio_description: Listen for excessively loud, overly expressive, or rapid vocalizations. The caregiver's speech follows too fast without pausing for infant reactions. The vocal quality is characterized by tension and agitation — the caregiver may be too loud and too expressive regardless of the infant's behavioral signals of discomfort or withdrawal.

Chos.label: Hostile
Chos.full_description: The adult's engagement with the baby is characterized by hostile affect/behavior. The adult's facial expressions are stressed out, upset or aggressive. Vocalizations are high pitched or the voice is cracking. The mother is curt with the baby, pushes him/her, drags or pulls him/her or her actions and vocalizations follow one another staccato-like. She makes fun of her baby, for example by imitating his/her vocalizations or mimic expressions.
Chos.video_description: Look for stressed out, upset, or aggressive facial expressions. The caregiver is curt with the baby, may push, drag, or pull the infant. Actions follow one another in a staccato-like pattern. The caregiver may make fun of the baby by mockingly imitating the infant's facial expressions.
Chos.audio_description: Listen for high-pitched vocalizations or a cracking voice. The vocal quality is hostile, curt, and staccato-like. The caregiver may mock or imitate the infant's vocalizations in a derisive manner.

Cnon.label: Non-Infant Focused Engagement
Cnon.full_description: The adult is not attending to the baby and is involved in a non-infant focused activity (e.g., fixing his/her clothing, talking to the experimenter, looking at an object the infant is not looking at, rubbing eyes and face because of tiredness).
Cnon.video_description: The adult is not visually attending to the baby. The caregiver may be fixing their own clothing, looking at non-infant-related objects, rubbing their eyes or face due to tiredness, or otherwise engaged in a non-infant focused activity.
Cnon.audio_description: The adult may be talking to the experimenter or someone other than the baby, or may be silent while engaged in a non-infant activity. The vocalizations, if present, are not directed at the infant.

Cneu.label: Social Monitor / No Vocs or Neutral Vocs
Cneu.full_description: The adult watches or focuses his/her attention on the baby or the baby's activities while his/her facial expressions are neutral. The caregiver may look interested in the infant and may occasionally show a hint of a smile. The adult may touch the baby. The adult can be silent or vocalize to the baby in a neutral manner. Do not code brief conversational pauses (no vocs with neutral face) as this code unless these pauses last 2 seconds or more. Comforting sounds like shhh are coded here. If the caregiver's face is neutral but he/she is speaking in Motherese or in a positive tone of voice, score Social Monitor/Positive Vocs instead. In cases where it is difficult to differentiate between the Negative Engagement codes (particularly Withdrawn) and Social Monitor/No Vocs or Neutral Vocs, score Social Monitor. Similarly if it is difficult to differentiate between Social Monitor/Neutral and Social Monitor/Positive Vocs, score Social Monitor/No Vocs or Neutral Vocs.
Cneu.video_description: Look for the adult watching or focusing attention on the baby or the baby's activities with neutral facial expressions. The caregiver may look interested and may occasionally show a hint of a smile. The adult may touch the baby. In ambiguous cases: if difficult to differentiate between Withdrawn and Social Monitor, score Social Monitor. If difficult to differentiate between Social Monitor/Neutral and Social Monitor/Positive Vocs, score Social Monitor/Neutral.
Cneu.audio_description: The adult is silent or vocalizing to the baby in a neutral manner. Comforting sounds like 'shhh' are coded here. Do not code brief conversational pauses unless they last 2 seconds or more. If the caregiver's vocalizations are in Motherese or a positive tone of voice while the face is neutral, score Social Monitor/Positive Vocs instead.

Cpvc.label: Social Monitor / Positive Vocs
Cpvc.full_description: The caregiver's gaze is focused on the infant or on the infant's activities. His/her facial expressions are neutral, interested, and may occasionally show a hint of a smile. Although the caregiver's face is neutral, his/her vocalizations are positive (e.g., he/she may use Motherese, make kissing or clicking sounds, sing). In cases where it is difficult to differentiate between Social Monitor/Positive Vocs and Social Positive Engagement, score Social Monitor/Positive Vocs.
Cpvc.video_description: Look for the caregiver's gaze focused on the infant or the infant's activities with neutral or interested facial expressions that may occasionally show a hint of a smile. The face is neutral — not displaying full smiles, laughter, or play faces. In ambiguous cases between Social Monitor/Positive Vocs and Social Positive Engagement, score Social Monitor/Positive Vocs.
Cpvc.audio_description: Listen for positive vocalizations such as Motherese, kissing or clicking sounds, or singing. Although the caregiver's face is neutral, the vocal quality is warm, positive and infant-directed. The key differentiator is that the vocalizations are positive while the facial expression remains neutral or only hints at a smile.

Cpos.label: Social Positive Engagement
Cpos.full_description: The caregiver expresses positive affect such as full smiles (closed or open), laughter, or play faces. The adult may vocalize to the baby using Motherese or sing but there is nothing exaggerated in his/her speaking or singing. The caregiver may play games with the infant but these games do not have a neutral quality. Instances when vocalizations (e.g., 'boop') make it physically impossible to smile are coded as Social Positive Engagement if they are preceded by Social Positive Engagement. If the 'boop' is preceded by a neutral face, code Social Monitor/Positive Vocs. If, after the vocalization, the caregiver's face is not positive (e.g., neutral), code the appropriate code. Similarly, if the caregiver covers his/her face or hides his/her face in the infant's body, code these instances as Social Positive Engagement if they are preceded by Social Positive Engagement. If the caregiver emerges with a non-positive face, code the appropriate code.
Cpos.video_description: Look for full smiles (closed or open), laughter, or play faces expressing positive affect. The caregiver may play games with the infant in a clearly positive manner. When vocalizations like 'boop' make it physically impossible to smile, code Social Positive Engagement only if preceded by Social Positive Engagement. If the caregiver covers or hides their face in the infant's body, code Social Positive Engagement if preceded by the same. If the caregiver emerges with a non-positive face, code the appropriate engagement phase.
Cpos.audio_description: Listen for laughter, warm Motherese, singing, or other clearly positive vocalizations. The vocal quality is joyful and engaging but not exaggerated. The caregiver uses infant-directed speech with positive affect. Positive vocalizations support this code when combined with visible positive facial expressions (full smiles, play faces).

Cusc.label: Unscorable
Cusc.full_description: If the adult's face is obscured because of poor camera angles or technical problems score Unscorable. Code all instances, however brief if the caregiver's face is completely obscured and if it is not possible to hear the caregiver's vocalizations. If it is possible to see part of the caregiver's face, code the appropriate engagement phase. Also, if you cannot see the face but can hear the caregiver talk using neutral or positive vocalizations, code Social Monitor/Neutral or Social Monitor/Positive Vocs.
Cusc.video_description: The adult's face is obscured because of poor camera angles or technical problems. If part of the caregiver's face is visible, code the appropriate engagement phase instead. Code Unscorable for all instances, however brief, when the face is completely obscured.
Cusc.audio_description: If the caregiver's face is not visible but you can hear neutral vocalizations, code Social Monitor/Neutral. If positive vocalizations are audible, code Social Monitor/Positive Vocs. Only score Unscorable when both the face is obscured and the vocalizations cannot be heard.
